question: In Liu et al. (2023), C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics were hot-pressed at 1600°C for 2 hours. What phase transformation of the sintering additive was achieved under these sintering conditions?
reference_answer: ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600°C for 2 hours. This conversion was confirmed by XRD results showing the disappearance of ZrSi2 peaks with the appearance of ZrC and SiC diffraction peaks.
answer_keys: almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC
evidence_any: The ball-milled C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders were hot pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement, load and temperature were recorded using TestMark to analyze the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate of 10 ◦C/min and then held for 30 min. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C following the same sintering process.

TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. Fig. 2 shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders and as-sintered C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics. As shown in Fig. 2, for the powders, the diffraction peaks of ZrSi2 and ZrB2 phases were clearly detected, while C was difficult to identify due to its amorphous state. After sintering at 1350–1600 ◦C, besides the primary ZrB2 phase, the diffraction peaks of ZrC and SiC appeared with the disappearance of ZrSi2 peaks, indicating the occurrence of the in-situ reactions mentioned above.
excerpts: The ball-milled C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders were hot pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement, load and temperature were recorded using TestMark to analyze the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate of 10 ◦C/min and then held for 30 min. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. In order to clarify the effect of sintering additive on the densification behavior and phase transition, pure ZrSi2 powders were also hot-pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C following the same sintering process.

TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. Fig. 2 shows X-ray diffraction patterns of the C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders and as-sintered C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics. As shown in Fig. 2, for the powders, the diffraction peaks of ZrSi2 and ZrB2 phases were clearly detected, while C was difficult to identify due to its amorphous state. After sintering at 1350–1600 ◦C, besides the primary ZrB2 phase, the diffraction peaks of ZrC and SiC appeared with the disappearance of ZrSi2 peaks, indicating the occurrence of the in-situ reactions mentioned above.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic sintered at 1600°C for 2 hours exhibited what high-temperature strength at 1800°C, and how did this compare to the undoped version?
reference_answer: The high-temperature flexural strength of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic at 1800°C was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of magnitude higher than the undoped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic at 34 ± 6 MPa. This improvement was attributed to the conversion of low-melting-point phases into refractory phases.
evidence_any: The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦C, 1450 ◦C, 1600 ◦C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600, respectively. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. Flexural strength of as-sintered ceramics was evaluated on a high-temperature mechanical testing machine under a vacuum condition using a 3-pt flexural test. The high-temperature furnace was equipped with C/C fixtures, and the testing temperature can reach up to 2000 ◦C. For the high-temperature testing, the samples were heated to 1800 ◦C at a heating rate of 15 ◦C/min and then held for 5 min to ensure the samples reached the test temperature.

The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa). Compared to its room-temperature strength of 494 ± 2 MPa, the high-temperature strength retention ratio of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).
excerpts: The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦C, 1450 ◦C, 1600 ◦C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600, respectively. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. Flexural strength of as-sintered ceramics was evaluated on a high-temperature mechanical testing machine under a vacuum condition using a 3-pt flexural test. The high-temperature furnace was equipped with C/C fixtures, and the testing temperature can reach up to 2000 ◦C. For the high-temperature testing, the samples were heated to 1800 ◦C at a heating rate of 15 ◦C/min and then held for 5 min to ensure the samples reached the test temperature.

The high-temperature flexural strength of ZC1600-2h was 390 ± 21 MPa, which was an order of magnitude higher than ZS15 (34 ± 6 MPa). Compared to its room-temperature strength of 494 ± 2 MPa, the high-temperature strength retention ratio of ZC1600-2h was as high as ~79%, which was much higher than that of ZS15 (~6%).

question: In Liu et al. (2023), when C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 powders were hot-pressed at 1600°C for 2 hours, what relative density was achieved, and what was the relationship between the microstructural phases and this densification outcome?
reference_answer: A fully-dense C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at 1600°C for 2 hours with a relative density of 100%. The complete conversion of ZrSi2 into SiC and ZrC particles during the sintering process promoted this full densification.
evidence_any: The ball-milled C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders were hot pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement, load and temperature were recorded using TestMark to analyze the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate of 10 ◦C/min and then held for 30 min. When the pressure was maintained after reaching 40 MPa, the shrinkage displacement increased steadily. Then, with the temperature increasing to 1600 ◦C, two distinct rapid densification stages were observed in the shrinkage curve.

TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. After sintering, the density of ceramics was determined by Archimedes method. The results showed that the relative density of ZC1600-2h was 100%, which meant that C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics was fully dense.
excerpts: The ball-milled C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 powders were hot pressed at 1350–1600 ◦C. During the hot-press sintering process, the instantaneous displacement, load and temperature were recorded using TestMark to analyze the densification behavior. The temperature profile was described as follows: it was increased to a certain sintering temperature with a heating rate of 10 ◦C/min and then held for 30 min. When the pressure was maintained after reaching 40 MPa, the shrinkage displacement increased steadily. Then, with the temperature increasing to 1600 ◦C, two distinct rapid densification stages were observed in the shrinkage curve.

TG-DSC, XRD and SEM results indicated that ZrSi2 was almost entirely converted into SiC and ZrC at 1600 ◦C for 2h, meanwhile, a fully-dense C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramic was obtained at such sintering conditions. After sintering, the density of ceramics was determined by Archimedes method. The results showed that the relative density of ZC1600-2h was 100%, which meant that C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics was fully dense.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), how did the microstructural morphology of C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramics evolve from 1350°C to 1600°C during hot-press sintering, and what was the final state of the Zr-Si-C phases?
reference_answer: The microstructure evolved from porous with loose ZrSi2-C mixture at 1350°C to a core-shell phase at 1600°C for 30 minutes, and finally to separated SiC and ZrC nanoparticles at 1600°C for 2 hours. This phase transformation enabled the formation of a fully dense ceramic with improved high-temperature properties.
answer_keys: porous surfaces with almost no pores
evidence_any: The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦C, 1450 ◦C, 1600 ◦C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600, respectively. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)).

After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr–Si–C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦C for 2 h, the core-shell phase was separated into small particles (as marked by the dashed line in Fig. 3(h)). Based on EDS analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3 (h)), the black and white phases were SiC and ZrC.
excerpts: The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1350 ◦C, 1450 ◦C, 1600 ◦C for 30 min were labeled as ZC1350, ZC1450, ZC1600, respectively. The C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at 1600 ◦C for 2 h was labeled as ZC1600-2h. Fig. 3 shows the typical morphologies on the polished surface of C-doped ZrB2–ZrSi2 ceramics sintered at different temperatures. As can be seen in Fig. 3(a), the polished surface of ZC1350 was quite rough with a lot of pores. When the sintering temperature increased to 1450 ◦C, the pores became less (Fig. 3(c)). In comparison, the polished surfaces of ZC1600 and ZC1600-2h were flat with almost no pores (Fig. 3(e, g)).

After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦C for 30 min, as shown in Fig. 3 (f), the Zr–Si–C cluster tfransformed into a core-shell phase (as marked by the dotted lines). Based on the micro-area analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3(f)), the core was carbide enriched Zr, and the shell was carbide enriched Si. After hot-sintering at 1600 ◦C for 2 h, the core-shell phase was separated into small particles (as marked by the dashed line in Fig. 3(h)). Based on EDS analysis (inserted image in Fig. 3 (h)), the black and white phases were SiC and ZrC.

question: In Liu et al. (2023), what was the significance of the two distinct rapid densification stages observed in the C-doped ZrB2-ZrSi2 ceramic, and what physical processes were responsible for each stage?
reference_answer: Two rapid densification stages occurred: the first at 1100-1300°C due to ZrSi2 softening that filled large pores among rigid ZrB2 powders, and the second at 1450-1600°C due to solid Si transitioning into Si-rich liquid phases that were extruded into small residual pores under applied pressure.
answer_keys: extruded from the Zr-Si-C clusters into the small residual pores
evidence_any: At the beginning of the densification process (~800 ◦C), the shrinkage displacement increased sharply, due to a uniaxial pressure of 40 MPa starting to be loaded at such temperature. When the pressure was maintained after reaching 40 MPa, the shrinkage displacement increased steadily. Then, with the temperature increasing to 1600 ◦C, two distinct rapid densification stages were observed in the shrinkage curve. First, at the temperature of 1100–1300 ◦C, the shrinkage rate significantly increased, while a phenomenon of load drop occurred simultaneously. After- ward, with the sintering temperature further increasing, the second rapid densification stage occurred at 1450–1600 ◦C, accompanying the fast shrinkage and load drop phenomena.

In the first rapid densification stage (1100–1300 ◦C), the sintering additive of ZrSi2 gradually became soft with the temperature increasing, which can be confirmed by the shrinkage curves of ZrSi2 ceramic. Therefore, due to the good plasticity at high temperature, ZrSi2 rapidly filled in the large pores among the rigid ZrB2 powders. With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr–Si–C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure.
excerpts: At the beginning of the densification process (~800 ◦C), the shrinkage displacement increased sharply, due to a uniaxial pressure of 40 MPa starting to be loaded at such temperature. When the pressure was maintained after reaching 40 MPa, the shrinkage displacement increased steadily. Then, with the temperature increasing to 1600 ◦C, two distinct rapid densification stages were observed in the shrinkage curve. First, at the temperature of 1100–1300 ◦C, the shrinkage rate significantly increased, while a phenomenon of load drop occurred simultaneously. After- ward, with the sintering temperature further increasing, the second rapid densification stage occurred at 1450–1600 ◦C, accompanying the fast shrinkage and load drop phenomena.

In the first rapid densification stage (1100–1300 ◦C), the sintering additive of ZrSi2 gradually became soft with the temperature increasing, which can be confirmed by the shrinkage curves of ZrSi2 ceramic. Therefore, due to the good plasticity at high temperature, ZrSi2 rapidly filled in the large pores among the rigid ZrB2 powders. With the temperature increasing higher than 1410 ◦C, the solid Si transited into Si-rich liquid phases, which were extruded from the Zr–Si–C clusters into the small residual pores among ZrB2 particles under applied pressure.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), how did the porosity gradient in porous graded ZrB2 ceramics vary across the sample thickness when sintered at different holding temperatures using the modified asymmetric die configuration?
reference_answer: Porous graded ZrB2 samples produced using the asymmetric die showed porosity variation up to about 26 vol.% across the sample volume. At 1600°C, maximum porosity of about 26 vol.% was achieved on the bottom side while the top remained highly consolidated, with the porosity trend becoming more uniform at higher temperatures like 1750°C.
answer_keys: porosity changes up to about 26 vol.%
maximum porosity levels of about 26 vol.%
highly consolidated on the top end
evidence_any: Porous graded materials (PGMs) consisting of ZrB2 are obtained by Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) taking advantage of an asymmetric die configuration with changing cross section. Due to the temperature gradient established along the longitudinal direction, porosity changes up to about 26 vol.% were generated across the volume. The surface roughness of the resulting products increased as the holding temperature was reduced. Superior solar absorbance and lower spectral selectivity properties were correspondingly obtained.

The PGM produced at 1500 ◦C displays high porosity on both sides. On the other hand, SPSed samples obtained at 1600 and 1750°C are highly consolidated on the top end, whereas maximum porosity levels of about 26 and 10 vol.% are achieved on the opposite side, respectively. Based on these results, it can be stated that porous graded microstructures are always generated within the temperature conditions taken into account.
excerpts: Porous graded materials (PGMs) consisting of ZrB2 are obtained by Spark Plasma Sintering (SPS) taking advantage of an asymmetric die configuration with changing cross section. Due to the temperature gradient established along the longitudinal direction, porosity changes up to about 26 vol.% were generated across the volume. The surface roughness of the resulting products increased as the holding temperature was reduced. Superior solar absorbance and lower spectral selectivity properties were correspondingly obtained.

The PGM produced at 1500 ◦C displays high porosity on both sides. On the other hand, SPSed samples obtained at 1600 and 1750°C are highly consolidated on the top end, whereas maximum porosity levels of about 26 and 10 vol.% are achieved on the opposite side, respectively. Based on these results, it can be stated that porous graded microstructures are always generated within the temperature conditions taken into account.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), what was the relationship between the temperature gradient established during SPS processing of porous graded ZrB2 ceramics and the resulting porosity distribution across the sample?
reference_answer: The asymmetric die configuration created a temperature gradient along the longitudinal direction during SPS processing. This temperature variation generated porosity gradients in the ZrB2 material, with lower temperatures at the bottom of the die resulting in higher porosity levels compared to the hotter top section.
answer_keys: temperature gradient established along the longitudinal direction
temperature differences established across the graded structures
porosity degree progressively increases when moving downwards
evidence_any: The dies used for the preparation of standard and porous graded ZrB2 products are schematically shown in Fig. 1. The modified (M) graphite tool specifically proposed in this work for the preparation of PGMs displays an asymmetric geometry, where the external diameter of the cross section of the mold varies from 30 to 35mm. During each SPS run, the temperature on the 30 mm section die was increased to the maximum value in 10 min and then the latter level was kept for 20 min. The effect of TD was investigated in the range 1500–1750 ◦C.

It is clearly seen that the sample region positioned on the top side is highly densified, with only a small amount of residual pores. On the other hand, the porosity degree progressively increases when moving downwards across the specimen. Therefore, it is possible to state that the temperature distribution established within the (M)-type tool adopted in this work was sufficient for the obtainment of a porosity gradient in the ZrB2 sample.
excerpts: The dies used for the preparation of standard and porous graded ZrB2 products are schematically shown in Fig. 1. The modified (M) graphite tool specifically proposed in this work for the preparation of PGMs displays an asymmetric geometry, where the external diameter of the cross section of the mold varies from 30 to 35mm. During each SPS run, the temperature on the 30 mm section die was increased to the maximum value in 10 min and then the latter level was kept for 20 min. The effect of TD was investigated in the range 1500–1750 ◦C.

It is clearly seen that the sample region positioned on the top side is highly densified, with only a small amount of residual pores. On the other hand, the porosity degree progressively increases when moving downwards across the specimen. Therefore, it is possible to state that the temperature distribution established within the (M)-type tool adopted in this work was sufficient for the obtainment of a porosity gradient in the ZrB2 sample.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), how did the surface roughness of porous graded ZrB2 ceramics correlate with the holding temperature used during SPS processing?
reference_answer: Samples sintered at lower holding temperatures exhibited relatively higher values of both average roughness (Ra) and total roughness (Rt). This outcome was consistent with the higher porosity degree and more irregular surfaces displayed by those specimens produced at 1500 and 1600°C compared to those at 1750°C.
answer_keys: higher values of both average roughness
surface roughness increased as the holding temperature was reduced
evidence_any: The surfaces (bottom side) of each sintered specimen to be characterized from microstructural, topological and optical points of view were first ground and then polished with diamond paste down to 0.25 μm. The topological characterization of such specimens were further investigated through roughness measurements. Samples sintered at lower TD exhibited relatively higher values of both average roughness, Ra, and total roughness, Rt. This outcome is consistent with SEM observations and could be mainly explained with the higher porosity degree correspondingly displayed by those specimens.

Significantly diverse microstructures can be observed, depending on the TD value adopted during the SPS process. Specifically, highly porous and irregular surfaces are formed when operating at 1500 and 1600 ◦C. On the other hand, when the dwell temperature was increased to 1750 ◦C, the surface of the corresponding material became relatively smoother (Fig. 6(c)), although still rather porous.
excerpts: The surfaces (bottom side) of each sintered specimen to be characterized from microstructural, topological and optical points of view were first ground and then polished with diamond paste down to 0.25 μm. The topological characterization of such specimens were further investigated through roughness measurements. Samples sintered at lower TD exhibited relatively higher values of both average roughness, Ra, and total roughness, Rt. This outcome is consistent with SEM observations and could be mainly explained with the higher porosity degree correspondingly displayed by those specimens.

Significantly diverse microstructures can be observed, depending on the TD value adopted during the SPS process. Specifically, highly porous and irregular surfaces are formed when operating at 1500 and 1600 ◦C. On the other hand, when the dwell temperature was increased to 1750 ◦C, the surface of the corresponding material became relatively smoother (Fig. 6(c)), although still rather porous.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), how did the solar absorbance and spectral selectivity properties of porous graded ZrB2 ceramics produced at 1500°C compare to dense ZrB2 and the reference solar absorber material silicon carbide?
reference_answer: The porous graded ZrB2 sample sintered at 1500°C displayed higher solar absorbance similar to SiC and exhibited an improved spectral selectivity of 1.5 compared to SiC's selectivity of 1. This demonstrated superior optical performance for solar receiver applications compared to the conventional dense ZrB2 and the established SiC standard.
answer_keys: similar value for alpha and an even improved spectral selectivity
evidence_any: As a term of comparison, it should be noted that silicon carbide, which has been under development for solar absorbers for several years, is characterized by alpha and alpha/epsilon values of approximately 0.8 and 1, respectively. Therefore, all the ZrB2 samples investigated in the present work display higher solar selectivity with respect to SiC, although the solar absorbance is relatively lower as compared to the reference material.

The PGM product sintered at TD = 1500 ◦C and characterized by relatively higher surface roughness and porosity shows a similar value for alpha and an even improved spectral selectivity (1.5) with respect to SiC. These outcomes are important and provide relevant indications for possible future exploitation of novel solar absorbers based on UHTCs.
excerpts: As a term of comparison, it should be noted that silicon carbide, which has been under development for solar absorbers for several years, is characterized by alpha and alpha/epsilon values of approximately 0.8 and 1, respectively. Therefore, all the ZrB2 samples investigated in the present work display higher solar selectivity with respect to SiC, although the solar absorbance is relatively lower as compared to the reference material.

The PGM product sintered at TD = 1500 ◦C and characterized by relatively higher surface roughness and porosity shows a similar value for alpha and an even improved spectral selectivity (1.5) with respect to SiC. These outcomes are important and provide relevant indications for possible future exploitation of novel solar absorbers based on UHTCs.

question: In Licheri et al. (2019), what was the initial composition and synthesis method used to prepare the ZrB2 powders, and what phase confirmation was obtained after the SHS process?
reference_answer: ZrB2 was synthesized by Self-propagating High-temperature Synthesis (SHS) from commercial zirconium and amorphous boron powders with a small excess of boron. XRD analysis confirmed complete conversion of the initial reactants to hexagonal ZrB2 was achieved during the synthesis process, after which 20 minutes of milling converted the SHS samples into powder form.
answer_keys: complete conversion of initial reactants to hexagonal ZrB2
XRD analysis patterns confirm complete conversion
evidence_any: Commercial zirconium (Alfa Aesar GmbH & Co KG, Karlsruhe, Germany, particle size < 45 μm, 98.5% purity) and amorphous boron (Sigma-Aldrich; St. Louis, Mo, USA, particle size ≤1 μm, ≥95% purity) powders were used for the preparation of ZrB2 by SHS. As described elsewhere, the small excess of B with respect to the stoichiometric quantity was needed to balance the amount of this element consumed for reducing ZrO2 impurity initially present on the surface of Zr powders. Reactants mixing was carried out for 20min using a SPEX 8000 shaker mill.

The first processing step was aimed to the preparation of zirconium diboride by SHS. The XRD analysis patterns confirm that the complete conversion of initial reactants to hexagonal ZrB2 was achieved during the synthesis process. A 20 min milling treatment was then carried out to convert SHS samples in powder form. The obtained powders, which displayed an average particles size of 6.7 ± 0.4 μm as measured by laser light scattering analysis, were then processed by SPS.
excerpts: Commercial zirconium (Alfa Aesar GmbH & Co KG, Karlsruhe, Germany, particle size < 45 μm, 98.5% purity) and amorphous boron (Sigma-Aldrich; St. Louis, Mo, USA, particle size ≤1 μm, ≥95% purity) powders were used for the preparation of ZrB2 by SHS. As described elsewhere, the small excess of B with respect to the stoichiometric quantity was needed to balance the amount of this element consumed for reducing ZrO2 impurity initially present on the surface of Zr powders. Reactants mixing was carried out for 20min using a SPEX 8000 shaker mill.

The first processing step was aimed to the preparation of zirconium diboride by SHS. The XRD analysis patterns confirm that the complete conversion of initial reactants to hexagonal ZrB2 was achieved during the synthesis process. A 20 min milling treatment was then carried out to convert SHS samples in powder form. The obtained powders, which displayed an average particles size of 6.7 ± 0.4 μm as measured by laser light scattering analysis, were then processed by SPS.
